design_philosophy.core_loop: Govern provinces of China through turn-based strategic decision-making across multiple domains: economics, diplomacy, intrigue, warfare, and covert operations
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing economic management, political maneuvering, and military conquest to achieve empire expansion while competing against rival factions
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a warlord or strategist commanding an empire during the Three Kingdoms period, wielding both sword and intrigue to conquer and rule China
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rejects pure military conquest as the only path to victory. Emphasizes that 'there are many ways beyond simple conquest to accomplish one's goals'—economics, bribery, coercion, diplomacy, and covert action are equally valid and celebrated as core mechanics rather than supplementary systems

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency through strategic choice and long-term planning; players can pursue different victory conditions and employ varied economic/political/military strategies to adapt difficulty to playstyle

progression.upgrade_system: Province governance and resource accumulation; economic development, army building, and political influence expansion as soft progression mechanisms
progression.unlock_mechanism: Control and pacification of provinces unlocks new territories and strategic options; diplomatic relationships unlock alliance and trading opportunities
replayability_hooks: Different starting factions with unique strategic positions
Multiple playstyles encouraged (economic vs. military vs. diplomatic)

world_building_techniques: Historical setting (Three Kingdoms period of China)
Economic and political simulation grounding narrative in systemic storytelling
Named provinces and historical context implied through gameplay mechanics

faction_indicators: Rival warlords competing for territorial control
Economic competition for resources and trade routes
danger_signals: Coup attempts and internal intrigue from bribed officials
environmental_storytelling: Economic indicators show faction strength and stability
Diplomatic agreements and betrayals drive narrative tension

sources_used: Wikipedia: Romance of the Three Kingdoms (video game)
